pages.raw_text:
The national and state conserva-
ton commissions are engaged in
work of education of inestimable
value to the people of the whole
country. The necessity for the con-
servation of our natural resources
was never fully understood until
these commissions began the work
which they now have in mind.
Through their efforts the people
have recently learned that the
mineral resources, which cannot be
renewed, are in course of exhaust
ion, and, unless something is done
to reforest the deforested areas and
replenish the timber supply the
time is not far distant when there
will be a fuel famine with all that
it implies. The destruction OF the
forests means more than the loss
of one of the most valuable of all
resources. 1t means, as well, the
possible depletion of the waters in
the navigable streams and the loss
of the magnificent water power
that tributary streams now furnish
for industrial commercial and irri-
cation purposes. The conference
between the national and state
commissions on the conservation of
our natural resources means the
taking of an inventory of all that
remains of a splendid heritage that
has been bequeathed to us by our
forefathers and the devising Of
some scheme of administration
which will protect what is left, and
renew those resources which are
capable of renewal by proper and
concerted effort


1t is my opinion that the best
administrative policy is to be found
in the enactment of federal law
covering all that comes within the
jurisdiction of the national con-
gress. The resources, about which
there can be no question. are the
forests and mineral resources
within that portion of the public
domain which remains undisposed
of and the navigable waterways
and the tributary streams in SO far
as their depletion or diversion
might affect navigation.


am an advocate of federal juris-
diction over the natural resources
of the country insofar as that juris-
diction can be extended, because,
with the diversity of interests in
the several states, the conflicting
laws, as well as the unreconcilable
judicial decisions, it is practically
impossible for the states to engage
in the work of conservation with
that energy and determination
which are necessary to attain the
best results.


1t is we'll understood that private
individuals and corporations have
secured in many states magnificent
water powers and valuable mineral
deposits, and these interests, to-
gether with the conflict in the laws
and judicial decisions, make it prac-
tically impossible to secure uni
